reasoning: The 180-day SOFR average is strongly trending downward due to the mechanical effect of dropping older, higher daily rates from late 2024 and replacing them with current, lower daily rates. Because it is a 180-day average, it possesses significant inertia. For the June dates, it is mathematically near-impossible for the average to increase, barring an unprecedented massive off-cycle rate hike by the Fed. By August, it would require significant and unexpected rate hikes to reverse the downward momentum. By November, the 180-day average will fully reflect rates from May to November; since current daily rates are below the May 25 average, an increase by November would still require the Federal Reserve to shift unexpectedly to rate hikes, which has a moderate but minority probability. Therefore, probabilities increase with the time horizon (to account for uncertainty on Fed policy later in the year) but remain solidly in the 'no' direction.
